perturbation.original_text: At any time upon the request of the Company for any reason, you shall (and will direct your Representatives to) promptly, and in any event no later than 10 days after receipt of the request, deliver to the Company, or at your option, destroy, all Evaluation Material (including all copies, extracts and other reproductions thereof, whether in paper, electronic or other form or media) furnished to you or your Representatives by or on behalf of the Company pursuant to this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: At any time upon the request of the Company for any reason, you shall (and will direct your Representatives to) promptly, and in any event no later than 3 days after receipt of the request, deliver to the Company, or at your option, destroy, all Evaluation Material (including all copies, extracts and other reproductions thereof, whether in paper, electronic or other form or media) furnished to you or your Representatives by or on behalf of the Company pursuant to this Agreement
perturbation.explanation: Shortening the deadline to return or destroy Evaluation Material to 3 days may be considered unreasonably short, especially considering the volume and complexity of the information, potentially conflicting with laws or regulations concerning reasonable timeframes for compliance. This introduces a legal contradiction by potentially violating standards of reasonableness implicit in contract law and fair business practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text may be deemed an unfair business practice under 15 U.S. Code § 45(a)(1) if the timeframe is unreasonably short, hindering a party's ability to reasonably comply. While this law doesn't specify exact timeframes, courts often consider 'reasonableness' in business dealings.

perturbation.justification: The modified text shortens the timeframe for returning or destroying evaluation material from 10 days to 3 days. This change introduces a potential legal contradiction as the shortened timeframe may be considered unreasonable, thereby potentially conflicting with 15 U.S. Code § 45(a)(1) regarding unfair methods of competition. The snippet provided states: "(a) Declaration of unlawfulness; power to prohibit unfair practices; inapplicability to foreign trade (1) Unfair methods of competition". While the law doesn't explicitly define 'reasonableness', the modified text, by reducing the timeframe to 3 days, could be interpreted as an unfair practice if it hinders the recipient's ability to reasonably comply, thus contradicting the principle outlined in the law.

perturbation.original_text:
You agree that for a period of eighteen (18) months from and after the date hereof (the “ Standstill Period ”), neither you nor any of your affiliates, directly or indirectly, shall:
(a) acquire, offer to acquire, or agree to acquire, whether by means of purchase or otherwise, any (i) securities (or any interest therein or right thereto) having statutory, organic or contractual voting power, whether or not contingent or upon exercise, conversion or exchange (“ Voting Securities ”), of the Company or (ii) assets or businesses of the Company or of any division or operating unit of the Company
perturbation.changed_text:
You agree that for a period of five (5) years from and after the date hereof (the “ Standstill Period ”), neither you nor any of your affiliates, directly or indirectly, shall:
(a) acquire, offer to acquire, or agree to acquire, whether by means of purchase or otherwise, any (i) securities (or any interest therein or right thereto) having statutory, organic or contractual voting power, whether or not contingent or upon exercise, conversion or exchange (“ Voting Securities ”), of the Company or (ii) assets or businesses of the Company or of any division or operating unit of the Company
perturbation.explanation: Extending the standstill period to five years could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade, potentially violating antitrust laws. While NDAs can have standstill provisions, overly long or broad restrictions might be challenged as anti-competitive.
perturbation.law_explanation: A five-year standstill period might be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade under the Sherman Act (15 U.S.C. § 1), especially if it significantly limits GTCR LLC's ability to invest in or acquire similar businesses, thereby reducing competition.  The reasonableness depends on specific market conditions and the scope of the restriction.

Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the standstill period from 18 months to 5 years. The Law Explanation states that a five-year standstill period might be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade under the Sherman Act (15 U.S.C. § 1). Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 1, states that "Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal." The Perturbation Explanation also indicates that extending the standstill period to five years could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade, potentially violating antitrust laws. Therefore, the change creates a legal contradiction by potentially violating antitrust laws related to restraint of trade.

perturbation.original_text: You agree that, except as provided in a definitive agreement relating to a Transaction, for a period of one (1) year following the date of this Agreement, you shall not, and you shall not authorize, instruct, encourage or facilitate the ability of any of your Representatives and any person acting on behalf of to, in any manner, directly or indirectly, solicit for hire any of the officers or employees of the Company with whom you had contact or first became aware of during the process contemplated herein; provided, however, that the foregoing does not preclude you or your Representatives from: (a) soliciting employees through, or hiring employees who respond to, general job advertisements or similar notices that are not targeted specifically at the employees of the Company; or (b) soliciting or hiring employees whose employment has been terminated by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: You agree that, except as provided in a definitive agreement relating to a Transaction, for a period of three (3) years following the date of this Agreement, you shall not, and you shall not authorize, instruct, encourage or facilitate the ability of any of your Representatives and any person acting on behalf of to, in any manner, directly or indirectly, solicit for hire any of the officers or employees of the Company with whom you had contact or first became aware of during the process contemplated herein; provided, however, that the foregoing does not preclude you or your Representatives from: (a) soliciting employees through, or hiring employees who respond to, general job advertisements or similar notices that are not targeted specifically at the employees of the Company; or (b) soliciting or hiring employees whose employment has been terminated by the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to three years may be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on employee mobility, potentially conflicting with California law, which strongly disfavors restrictions on an individual's ability to pursue their chosen profession. Although the agreement is governed by Delaware law, a court may consider California's public policy if the agreement is significantly connected to California.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business.  A three-year non-solicitation agreement could be challenged as an unlawful restraint, particularly if it significantly hinders employees' career prospects.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period from one year to three years. According to the Law Explanation, California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business, and a three-year non-solicitation agreement could be challenged as an unlawful restraint. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The Perturbation Explanation also highlights that extending the period to three years may be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on employee mobility, potentially conflicting with California law. Therefore, the extended non-solicitation period in the changed text directly contradicts California law prohibiting restraints on trade, making the contradiction direct, enforceable, and serious.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the internal procedural and substantive laws of the State of Delaware, without reference to the conflict of law principles of that state.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the People's Republic of China, without reference to the conflict of law principles of that jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to that of the People's Republic of China could create enforceability issues in a US court, particularly regarding provisions related to data protection, freedom of information, and intellectual property, where PRC laws may differ significantly from US legal principles. This could be considered against public policy in certain US jurisdictions, impacting the agreement's validity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Public Policy Exception to Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose the governing law, the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 allows a court to disregard the chosen law if it violates a fundamental public policy of the state where the court is located. Selecting PRC law could conflict with US public policy, especially concerning intellectual property and data protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: W:\pennlaw\bll\archives\findingaids\PU-L.ALI.10.002-ead.xml Guide to the Mary Kay Kane Papers (Complex Litigation Project) PU-L.ALI.10.002 Finding aid prepared by Jordon Steele University of Pennsylvania: Biddle Law Library: American Law Institute Archives 3460 Chestnut Street Biddle Law Library Philadelphia, PA, 19104-3406 215-898-5011 2010 This finding aid was produced using the Archivists' Toolkit 2011-04-28T16:31-0400 English Describing Archives: A Content Standard Mary Kay Kane Papers (Complex Litigation Project) PU-L.ALI.10.002 University of Pennsylvania: Biddle Law Library: American Law Institute Archives 1.0 linear foot 1988-1993 Mary Kay Kane graduated from University of Michigan Law School in 1971. She served as a Professor at the State University of New York - Buffalo Law School from 1974 to 1977 and as a professor, Academic Dean, Dean, and Chancellor at the University of California, Hastings Law School from 1977 to 2006. Kane's area of interest was civil procedure from which she published many books and articles. She served as the Assistant Reporter for the American Law Institute Complex Litigation Project.The Mary Kay Kane Papers (Complex Litigation Project), 1988-1993, include correspondence and meeting transcripts related to Kane's work as Assistant Reporter on the American Law Institute (ALI) Complex Litigation Project, published in 1994 as Complex Litigation: Statutory Recommendations and Analysis. The majority of the series is correspondence regarding drafts, advising, and the Choice of Law clause. The meeting transcripts include transcripts of the ALI annual meetings and consultative meetings from 1989 to 1990. Kane, Mary Kay Bibliography Mary Kay Kane, Emeritus Professor of Law. University of California, Hastings. http://www.uchastings.edu/faculty-administration/ faculty/kane/index.html (Accessed February 18, 2010). Biographical/Historical note Mary Kay Kane was born and raised in Detroit, Michigan. She attended the University of Michigan for her B.A. in English, graduating in 1968, and graduated from the University of Michigan Law School with her J.D. with honors in 1971. After commencement, she became the Co-Director of the National Science Foundation project on confidentiality, privacy, and social science research working both at University of Michigan and Harvard Law School. In 1974, Kane started teaching at the State University of New York at Buffalo Law School as an Assistant Professor. She left SUNY-Buffalo Law School to become a professor at University of California, Hastings Law School in 1977. Kane was Assistant Academic Dean from 1980 to 1982, Acting Academic Dean from 1987 to 1988, Academic Dean from 1990 to 1993 and Dean from 1993 to 2006 at California, Hastings Law School. She also served as Chancellor from 2000 to 2006 and has taught as a visiting professor at University of Michigan, Boalt Hill, and the University of Texas at Austin. Kane has written many books and articles on civil procedure of law. She served as Assistant Reporter for the American Law Institute Complex Litigation Project, of which she is a member. In 2001, Kane held the position of President of the Association of American Law Schools. She is also a member of the Council of the ABA Section on Legal Education and Admissions to the Bar and a member of the Standing Committee on Practice and Procedure for the United States Judicial Conference. Scope and Contents note The Mary Kay Kane Papers (Complex Litigation Project), 1988-1993, include correspondence and meeting transcripts related to Kane's work as Assistant Reporter on the American Law Institute's (ALI) Complex Litigation Project, which was published in 1994 as Complex Litigation: Statutory Recommendations and Analysis. The majority of the series is correspondence regarding drafts, advising, and the Choice of Law provision. The meeting transcripts include transcripts of the ALI annual meetings and consultative meetings from 1989 to 1990. Processing Information note Process
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces Delaware law with the law of the People's Republic of China. The Law Explanation states that choosing PRC law could conflict with US public policy, especially concerning intellectual property and data protection. The Perturbation Explanation supports this, noting that enforcing the contract in a US court could be problematic, especially concerning data protection and intellectual property due to the differences between PRC and US laws, potentially violating public policy. This constitutes a legal contradiction under the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition, as the change introduces a governing law that may lead to terms that are unenforceable or illegal in the jurisdiction where the contract is being enforced.
